1: שָׂרַי אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ — "Sarai, wife of Abram, had not borne him children." The opening statement echoes Genesis 11:30 (Sarai was barren), restating the problem that the covenant promise of Genesis 15 has not resolved. שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית (šipḥāh miṣrît, Egyptian slave) — Hagar is identified by legal status and ethnicity. She is a šipḥāh (female slave, personal maid-servant) — legally subordinate to Sarai. The irony of her Egyptian identity is notable: she is presumably one of the servants acquired during the Egypt sojourn (Genesis 12:16), making the covenant couple's Egyptian interlude have an ongoing consequence in their household.
2: עֲצָרַנִי יְהוָה מִלֶּדֶת (ʿăṣāranî YHWH milleḏet) — "the LORD has shut me up from bearing." ʿĀṣar (to shut up, close, restrain) — the same verb applied to the LORD closing wombs in 1 Samuel 1:5-6 (Hannah) and elsewhere. Sarai attributes her barrenness directly to divine action — a theologically significant claim that neither narrator nor God challenges. אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה (ʾibbāneh mimmennāh, I will be built up through her) — the idiom for having children that plays on bānāh (build) and bēn (son): sons "build up" the family; to be barren is to be without a building, a demolished house. The surrogate-wife practice is attested in the Nuzi tablets (c. 1500-1400 BCE) and Code of Hammurabi §146: a wife who gives her slave to her husband for offspring retains legal superiority over the slave even after the birth.
3: וַתִּקַּח שָׂרַי אֵשֶׁת-אַבְרָם אֶת-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית שִׁפְחָתָהּ — Sarai is the grammatical subject: she takes, she gives. Abram is the passive recipient of his wife's initiative. לְאַבְרָם אִישָׁהּ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה — "to Abram her husband as a wife." Lĕʾiššāh (as a wife) — Hagar's legal status is temporarily elevated from šipḥāh (slave) to ʾiššāh (wife, secondary); she acquires wife-status specifically for the purpose of producing an heir. After the birth, the tension with Sarai will force a renegotiation of this status (Genesis 16:4-6; Genesis 21:10).
4: וַתַּהַר וַתֵּרֶא כִּי הָרָתָה וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ — "she conceived, and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress became light in her eyes." Tēqal (Qal imperfect of qālal, to be light, slight, contemptible) — from qālal, the opposite of kāḇēḏ (to be heavy/honored). Hagar honors her mistress less once she is pregnant — the pregnancy shifts the honor balance: the fertile servant now has something the barren mistress does not. The social dynamics of shame and honor are explicit: fertility is honor; barrenness is shame; Hagar's pregnancy reverses the conventional hierarchy.
5: חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ (ḥāmāsî ʿālêḵā) — "my wrong/violence is upon you." Ḥāmās (violence, wrongdoing, injustice) is a strong moral-legal term: it denotes the wickedness that filled the earth before the Flood (Genesis 6:11, 13), the crime of false witnesses (Exodus 23:1), and the violent oppression that the prophets condemn (Isaiah 53:9; Jeremiah 6:7; Ezekiel 7:23; Amos 3:10; Habakkuk 1:2-3). Sarai uses the most loaded moral language available to accuse Abram of wrongdoing. יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ — "the LORD judge between me and you" — Sarai invokes divine judgment on a domestic dispute, framing it as a legal matter before the covenant God.
6: עַשִׂי-לָהּ הַטּוֹב בְּעֵינָיִךְ — "do with her what is good in your eyes." Abram refuses to adjudicate and gives the decision back to Sarai — a moral abdication that parallels his silence during the Egypt episode (Genesis 12:10-20). Wayyîraʿ ʾōtāh Śāray — "Sarai mistreated/afflicted her." ʿĀnāh (Piel: to afflict, oppress, humble) — the same verb used for Egypt's oppression of Israel (Exodus 1:11, 12; Deuteronomy 26:6) and for the affliction the LORD predicts for Abram's descendants (Genesis 15:13: wĕʿinnû ʾōtām). Sarai's treatment of the Egyptian slave Hagar becomes an ironic anticipation of Egypt's treatment of the Hebrew slaves — and Hagar's flight anticipates the Exodus.
7: וַיִּמְצָאָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה עַל-עֵין הַמַּיִם — "the angel of the LORD found her at a spring of water." Malʾaḵ YHWH (the angel of the LORD) — the first appearance of this figure in the OT. The phrase presents a theologically complex figure: in the narrative, the angel speaks as YHWH (Genesis 16:10: "I will greatly multiply your offspring"), and Hagar identifies the one who spoke with ʾēl rōʾî (the God who sees me, Genesis 16:13). The angel appears to act with divine authority and is addressed as God — the pattern continues throughout the OT (Judges 6:11-23; Genesis 13:2-23; Exodus 3:2-6). The encounter with Hagar, an Egyptian slave fleeing into the desert, establishes the theological principle that YHWH's care extends beyond the covenant family.
8: מִזֶּה בָאת וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי — "where have you come from and where are you going?" The double interrogation parallels God's questions to Adam ("where are you?" Genesis 3:9), to Cain ("where is Abel your brother?" Genesis 4:9), to Elijah ("what are you doing here, Elijah?" 1 Kings 19:9, 13). The divine questions in the OT are not requests for information — the questioner knows — but are invitations to self-reflection and honest speech. Hagar's answer is honest: she is fleeing from her mistress. The angel does not rebuke her for the admission.
9: שׁוּבִי אֶל-גְּבִרְתֵּךְ וְהִתְעַנִּי תַּחַת יָדֶיהָ — "return to your mistress and submit under her hands." The command is morally difficult: Hagar is told to return to the source of her oppression. Hitchannî (Hithpael of ʿānāh, to humble yourself, submit) — the same root as the affliction of Genesis 16:6 (Sarai ʿinnatāh, afflicted her). Hagar is told to accept the humbling. The command is accompanied by a promise (Genesis 16:10) that makes the submission meaningful — not resignation to oppression but trust in divine provision beyond the immediate hardship.
10: הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֵךְ (harbāh ʾarbbeh ʾet-zarʿēḵ) — "I will greatly multiply your offspring." The infinitive absolute construction (same root doubled: harbāh ʾarbbeh) exactly parallels the offspring-multiplication promise to Abram (Genesis 17:2; Genesis 22:17) and to Israel (Exodus 1:7). Hagar receives the same promissory formula as the covenant patriarch — her descendants will be multiplied by divine commitment. This is extraordinary: a Gentile slave woman receives a form of the Abrahamic promise. Zaraʿ (offspring/seed) — the same collective noun as 3:15 and all the Abrahamic offspring promises.
11: יִשְׁמָעֵאל (Yišmaʿēʾl, Ishmael) — the name is a sentence: "God will hear" or "God has heard" (šāmaʿ + ʾēl). The naming-reason is explicit: kî-šāmaʿ YHWH ʾel-ʿonyēḵ (for the LORD has heard your affliction/misery). ʿOnî (misery, affliction) — the same root as ʿānāh (to afflict) in Genesis 16:6, 9. YHWH heard the affliction that Sarai caused (and that the LORD-directed sojourn indirectly produced through the Egypt episode). The name Ishmael encodes a theology of divine hearing extended to those outside the covenant line: God hears the slave.
12: פֶּרֶא אָדָם (pereh ʾāḏām, a wild-ass man) — Pereʾ is the wild ass of the steppe, whose freedom and defiance of domestication is celebrated in Job 39:5-8 ("I let the wild donkey go free; I untied its ropes; who gave the wild donkey its freedom? ... It ranges the hills for its pasture and searches for any green thing"). Ishmael will be untamed and free — a positive quality, not an insult. יָדוֹ בַכֹּל וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ — "his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him" — the wild-ass freedom manifests as universal conflict; Ishmael will be a fighting people, hostile to and hostiled by all. The oracle describes the Ishmaelite/Bedouin peoples of the desert, not a moral condemnation of Ishmael as an individual.
13: אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי (ʾattāh ʾēl rōʾî) — "You are El-Roi, the God of seeing/who sees me." Hagar gives God a name — one of the few instances where a human names God in the OT (cf. Abraham's YHWH-Yireh in Genesis 22:14). Rāʾāh (to see) — God saw Hagar in her affliction and heard (šāmaʿ) — the divine seeing and hearing form the theological content of the divine name she coins. הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי — "Have I actually seen here the one who sees me?" The Hebrew is difficult (literally: "after the one who sees me / have I also here seen?") — Hagar is astonished that she has seen God (or the divine representative) and survived; seeing God face-to-face was considered potentially fatal (Exodus 33:20; Judges 6:22-23; Genesis 13:22).
14: בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי (Bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî) — "the well of the Living One who sees me." The sanctuary name encodes the entire theophanic encounter: God is living (laḥay, the Living One), and he sees (rōʾî). The well becomes a named site that preserves the memory of YHWH's care for Hagar. It appears again in Genesis 24:62 (Isaac comes from there) and Genesis 25:11 (Isaac settled near it after Abraham's death) — the site of Hagar's theophany becomes part of the Isaacite landscape, without narrative commentary on the irony.
15: וַתֵּלֶד הָגָר לְאַבְרָם בֵּן — "Hagar bore to Abram a son." The birth is stated simply — the promised offspring arrives, though not the covenant offspring. וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָם שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה הָגָר יִשְׁמָעֵאל — Abram names the son Ishmael, fulfilling the angel's instruction (Genesis 16:11). The naming by Abram gives Ishmael patriarchal recognition — he is Abram's son, legally and socially, even if not the covenant heir. Abram's paternal act accepts responsibility for the child produced by Sarai's initiative and his own compliance.
16: וְאַבְרָם בֶּן-שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים — "and Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael." The chronological note anchors the birth of Ishmael precisely: 86 years old for Abram, ten years after arriving in Canaan (Genesis 16:3). The covenant promise of a son (Genesis 15:4) remains unfulfilled for another 13 years (Genesis 17:1: Abram is 99 when God next speaks; Genesis 17:25: Ishmael is 13 at the circumcision). The thirteen-year silence of YHWH between Ishmael's birth and the covenant of circumcision is not narrated — the narrator leaves it as an implied gap, a period of Abram living with the Ishmael solution as if it were sufficient.
